sections.title: Prediction markets in one sentence
sections.content: Prediction markets let participants trade contracts whose payouts depend on real-world outcomes — aggregating beliefs into prices that approximate probabilities. Liquidity and design matter: thin markets can swing wildly; deep markets often outperform polls for some event types.

modules.body: Prediction markets let participants trade contracts whose payouts depend on real-world outcomes — aggregating beliefs into prices that approximate probabilities. Liquidity and design matter: thin markets can swing wildly; deep markets often outperform polls for some event types.

sections.title: Why prices move
sections.content: Prices change as traders incorporate new information and as risk-averse participants demand compensation for holding risky positions. A 60-cent price does not mean “60% true” in every model — it reflects marginal trader beliefs plus fees and constraints.

modules.body: Prices change as traders incorporate new information and as risk-averse participants demand compensation for holding risky positions. A 60-cent price does not mean “60% true” in every model — it reflects marginal trader beliefs plus fees and constraints.

sections.title: Ezana context
sections.content: Platforms like Polymarket have popularized crypto-settled event markets. Always read the resolution rules: ambiguous wording causes disputes. The market price is only as good as the rulebook.

modules.body: Platforms like Polymarket have popularized crypto-settled event markets. Always read the resolution rules: ambiguous wording causes disputes. The market price is only as good as the rulebook.

quiz.question: A prediction market price can be interpreted as:
options: A market-implied probability blended with fees and risk premia
quiz.explanation: It is informative but not infallible.

quiz.question: Liquidity matters because:
options: Thin books mean small trades move prices more

quiz.question: Resolution rules matter because:
options: They define what counts as winning
quiz.explanation: Read the contract carefully before trading.

quiz.question: Why might prediction markets outperform simple polls?
options: Participants have financial incentives to be informed
quiz.explanation: Skin in the game can aggregate information.

quiz.question: New information tends to:
options: Move prices as traders update beliefs

sections.title: Decimal odds
sections.content: Decimal odds of 3.0 mean a winning one-unit stake returns three units total (profit plus stake). Implied probability is roughly 1/decimal for fair-ish markets before margin — bookmakers embed margin so implied probabilities sum above 100%.

modules.body: Decimal odds of 3.0 mean a winning one-unit stake returns three units total (profit plus stake). Implied probability is roughly 1/decimal for fair-ish markets before margin — bookmakers embed margin so implied probabilities sum above 100%.

sections.title: American odds
sections.content: Positive American odds (+200) show profit on a $100 stake; negative (-150) shows how much you must stake to win $100. Converting formats is essential when line shopping.

modules.body: Positive American odds (+200) show profit on a $100 stake; negative (-150) shows how much you must stake to win $100. Converting formats is essential when line shopping.

sections.title: Implied probability
sections.content: Implied probability is the break-even win rate needed ignoring fees. Comparing implied probability to your own estimate is the start of value betting — never the end.

modules.body: Implied probability is the break-even win rate needed ignoring fees. Comparing implied probability to your own estimate is the start of value betting — never the end.

quiz.question: Decimal odds of 2.5 on a winning bet return:
options: 2.5 units total including stake in typical display
quiz.explanation: Decimal includes stake in the return multiple (check your book’s display).

quiz.question: American odds of +300 imply a $100 bet wins how much profit if successful?
quiz.explanation: +300 means $300 profit on $100 stake at standard interpretation.

quiz.question: Bookmaker margin causes:
quiz.explanation: The overround is how books earn.

quiz.question: Implied probability helps you:
options: Compare market price to your own fair odds
quiz.explanation: Edge is the gap between belief and price — if you are calibrated.

quiz.question: Fractional odds 5/1 profit on a 1-unit stake is:
quiz.explanation: Classic fractional shows profit relative to stake.

sections.title: Spreads and moneylines
sections.content: Point spreads handicap favorites: covering means beating the spread after the adjustment. Moneylines pick winners outright with odds reflecting strength. Totals (over/under) bet combined scores. Parlays multiply payouts but multiply risk — each leg must win.

modules.body: Point spreads handicap favorites: covering means beating the spread after the adjustment. Moneylines pick winners outright with odds reflecting strength. Totals (over/under) bet combined scores. Parlays multiply payouts but multiply risk — each leg must win.

sections.title: House edge
sections.content: Sportsbooks embed margin in lines. Beating sports long-run requires finding mispriced lines — not picking winners more often in a naive sense. Bankroll discipline keeps you in the game while signal accumulates.

modules.body: Sportsbooks embed margin in lines. Beating sports long-run requires finding mispriced lines — not picking winners more often in a naive sense. Bankroll discipline keeps you in the game while signal accumulates.

sections.title: Variance
sections.content: Even positive-expectation bets can lose for long streaks due to variance. Short-term results are noisy; evaluate process over many bets.

modules.body: Even positive-expectation bets can lose for long streaks due to variance. Short-term results are noisy; evaluate process over many bets.

quiz.question: A favorite “covers the spread” when:
options: They win after applying the point spread adjustment
quiz.explanation: Spread betting is about margin of victory, not only W/L.

quiz.question: The sportsbook’s margin mainly:
options: Skews lines so implied probabilities include profit for the book
quiz.explanation: You are paying for market making and risk.

quiz.question: Why is “picking winners” alone a weak strategy?
options: Win rate without odds is meaningless
Edges live in price vs probability

quiz.question: Variance means:
options: Short-run outcomes can deviate wildly from expectation
quiz.explanation: Even good processes face losing streaks.

sections.title: Getting started safely
sections.content: Create an account on a reputable platform, enable two-factor authentication, and fund with amounts you can afford to lose. Read each market’s rules: some markets resolve on specific oracles or sources. Start small while learning order types and fees.

modules.body: Create an account on a reputable platform, enable two-factor authentication, and fund with amounts you can afford to lose. Read each market’s rules: some markets resolve on specific oracles or sources. Start small while learning order types and fees.

sections.title: Orders and liquidity
sections.content: Limit orders control price; market orders prioritize speed. In thin markets, market orders can slip badly. Watch the order book depth when sizing trades.

modules.body: Limit orders control price; market orders prioritize speed. In thin markets, market orders can slip badly. Watch the order book depth when sizing trades.

sections.title: Responsible participation
sections.content: Treat prediction markets as high-risk entertainment unless you have a verified edge and strict bankroll rules. Seek help if trading causes distress.

modules.body: Treat prediction markets as high-risk entertainment unless you have a verified edge and strict bankroll rules. Seek help if trading causes distress.

quiz.question: Before trading a Polymarket-style contract you should:
options: Read resolution criteria and fee schedule
quiz.explanation: Ambiguous rules cause disputes and losses.

quiz.question: Two-factor authentication helps because:
options: It adds a second layer beyond password theft

quiz.question: Market orders in illiquid books may:
options: Execute at worse prices than expected (slippage)

quiz.question: You should size positions:
options: With money you can afford to lose
quiz.explanation: Prediction markets are risky and can go to zero.

quiz.question: Limit orders help traders:
options: Control execution price at the risk of non-fill

sections.title: Crowd wisdom
sections.content: Aggregating diverse independent estimates can outperform individual experts under the right conditions — but markets need sufficient participation and incentives. Manipulation and thin liquidity break the magic.

modules.body: Aggregating diverse independent estimates can outperform individual experts under the right conditions — but markets need sufficient participation and incentives. Manipulation and thin liquidity break the magic.

sections.title: Information incorporation
sections.content: Prices should reflect available public information quickly when traders compete. If some participants have private information, markets may be less “fair” in the informational sense.

modules.body: Prices should reflect available public information quickly when traders compete. If some participants have private information, markets may be less “fair” in the informational sense.

sections.title: Limits
sections.content: Crowds can herd and panic. Financial markets and prediction markets both suffer bubbles. Use markets as inputs, not oracles of moral truth.

modules.body: Crowds can herd and panic. Financial markets and prediction markets both suffer bubbles. Use markets as inputs, not oracles of moral truth.

quiz.question: The “wisdom of crowds” works best when:
options: Information is secret to one person

quiz.question: Manipulation can break prediction markets when:
options: A trader can move prices with relatively small size

quiz.question: A fair market in the economic sense:
options: Still can be wrong ex post about outcomes

sections.title: Bankroll basics
sections.content: Your bankroll is the dedicated trading stake — separate from living expenses. Risking a tiny fraction per bet (often 1–2% or less of bankroll) prevents ruin during losing streaks. Chasing losses with larger bets is a common path to zero.

modules.body: Your bankroll is the dedicated trading stake — separate from living expenses. Risking a tiny fraction per bet (often 1–2% or less of bankroll) prevents ruin during losing streaks. Chasing losses with larger bets is a common path to zero.

sections.title: Kelly criterion (intro)
sections.content: Kelly sizing suggests bet size grows with edge and shrinks with odds — but real Kelly requires accurate probability estimates and can be volatile. Many pros use fractional Kelly to reduce variance.

modules.body: Kelly sizing suggests bet size grows with edge and shrinks with odds — but real Kelly requires accurate probability estimates and can be volatile. Many pros use fractional Kelly to reduce variance.

sections.title: Emotional discipline
sections.content: Tilt after losses leads to oversized bets. Pre-commit to rules and take breaks. Track results to see if your edge is real or imagined.

modules.body: Tilt after losses leads to oversized bets. Pre-commit to rules and take breaks. Track results to see if your edge is real or imagined.

quiz.question: Why do professionals avoid betting the rent?
options: Ruin risk dominates even with a small edge

quiz.question: Chasing losses typically:
options: Increases risk of ruin and emotional decisions

quiz.question: Fractional Kelly is often used because:
options: Full Kelly assumes perfect probabilities and can be very volatile

quiz.question: A 1% per-bet rule means:
options: Each bet risks ~1% of bankroll (definitions vary)
quiz.explanation: Common heuristic — adapt to your edge model.

quiz.question: Tracking results helps you:
options: Prove you have an edge or discover you do not

sections.title: What commodities are
sections.content: Commodities are fungible physical goods traded in standardized units: energy (oil, gas), metals (gold, copper), agriculture (corn, coffee). Prices balance global supply and demand with storage, transport, and geopolitics layered on top.

modules.body: Commodities are fungible physical goods traded in standardized units: energy (oil, gas), metals (gold, copper), agriculture (corn, coffee). Prices balance global supply and demand with storage, transport, and geopolitics layered on top.

sections.title: Why they matter to portfolios
sections.content: Commodities can diversify equity risk and sometimes hedge inflation — but they are volatile and can draw down for years. Role in a portfolio should match your horizon and risk tolerance.

modules.body: Commodities can diversify equity risk and sometimes hedge inflation — but they are volatile and can draw down for years. Role in a portfolio should match your horizon and risk tolerance.

sections.title: Spot vs futures
sections.content: Spot refers to immediate delivery markets; most retail exposure is via futures-based products or ETFs that roll contracts. Rolling introduces nuances like contango that affect returns.

modules.body: Spot refers to immediate delivery markets; most retail exposure is via futures-based products or ETFs that roll contracts. Rolling introduces nuances like contango that affect returns.

quiz.question: Commodities are generally:
options: Fungible bulk goods with standardized grades

quiz.question: Why might commodities diversify stocks?
options: Drivers differ — supply shocks, weather, geopolitics
They are the same asset class

quiz.question: Retail investors often gain exposure through:
quiz.explanation: Physical storage is costly; products abstract it.

quiz.question: Geopolitics can move commodity prices because:
options: Supply chains and sanctions affect availability
quiz.explanation: Energy and ag markets are politically sensitive.

quiz.question: “Fungible” means:
options: Units are interchangeable within a grade
quiz.explanation: Standard barrels or bushels trade interchangeably.

sections.title: Gold
sections.content: Gold is a monetary metal and jewelry/industrial input. It often behaves as a crisis and inflation hedge — imperfectly — and pays no coupon, so opportunity cost rises when real rates are high.

modules.body: Gold is a monetary metal and jewelry/industrial input. It often behaves as a crisis and inflation hedge — imperfectly — and pays no coupon, so opportunity cost rises when real rates are high.

sections.title: Oil
sections.content: Crude oil prices hinge on OPEC+ decisions, shale supply, refining constraints, and global demand. Inventory data releases routinely move markets.

modules.body: Crude oil prices hinge on OPEC+ decisions, shale supply, refining constraints, and global demand. Inventory data releases routinely move markets.

sections.title: Agriculture
sections.content: Weather dominates short-run crop prices; disease affects livestock. Seasonal planting and harvest patterns create recurring annual rhythms traders watch.

modules.body: Weather dominates short-run crop prices; disease affects livestock. Seasonal planting and harvest patterns create recurring annual rhythms traders watch.

quiz.question: Gold’s lack of coupon means:
options: Its opportunity cost rises when real interest rates are attractive elsewhere

quiz.question: Oil inventories often:
options: Move prices when they surprise versus expectations

quiz.question: Weather primarily impacts:
options: Agricultural yields and short-run ag prices
quiz.explanation: Crop stress and yields link to price.

quiz.question: OPEC+ coordination can influence oil by:
options: Setting retail gas prices directly in every country
quiz.explanation: Supply policy shifts the global balance.

quiz.question: Gold industrial and jewelry demand:
options: Contributes to baseline physical demand alongside investment flows

sections.title: Supply and demand
sections.content: Commodity prices clear markets where supply meets demand. Supply shocks (hurricanes, wars) can spike prices even without demand changes. Demand shocks (recessions) can crush energy use.

modules.body: Commodity prices clear markets where supply meets demand. Supply shocks (hurricanes, wars) can spike prices even without demand changes. Demand shocks (recessions) can crush energy use.

sections.title: Seasonality
sections.content: Agricultural markets show planting and harvest cycles. Natural gas has winter heating demand. Seasonality is a tendency, not a guarantee — exogenous shocks override patterns.

modules.body: Agricultural markets show planting and harvest cycles. Natural gas has winter heating demand. Seasonality is a tendency, not a guarantee — exogenous shocks override patterns.

sections.title: Inventories
sections.content: Low inventories amplify price volatility when supply wobbles. High inventories buffer shocks. Analysts track days of supply and stockpiles.

modules.body: Low inventories amplify price volatility when supply wobbles. High inventories buffer shocks. Analysts track days of supply and stockpiles.

quiz.question: A hurricane in the Gulf might:
options: Disrupt refining or production, affecting fuel prices

quiz.question: Seasonal patterns:
options: Provide tendencies that can be overridden by shocks

quiz.question: Recessions often reduce commodity demand because:
quiz.explanation: Industrial activity ties to energy and metals.

quiz.question: Inventories buffer prices by:
options: Allowing consumption without immediate production shocks

quiz.question: Demand elasticity varies:
options: By commodity — gasoline is inelastic short-run vs luxury goods

sections.title: Commodity ETFs
sections.content: ETFs like broad commodity funds or single-commodity trackers offer exposure without futures accounts — but returns may differ from spot due to rolling futures, fees, and tax treatment.

modules.body: ETFs like broad commodity funds or single-commodity trackers offer exposure without futures accounts — but returns may differ from spot due to rolling futures, fees, and tax treatment.

sections.title: ETNs and credit risk
sections.content: Exchange-traded notes are unsecured debt of the issuer — if the bank fails, you may lose money unrelated to the commodity. Read the prospectus.

modules.body: Exchange-traded notes are unsecured debt of the issuer — if the bank fails, you may lose money unrelated to the commodity. Read the prospectus.

sections.title: Equity producers
sections.content: Buying mining or oil company stocks gives commodity-linked exposure with corporate fundamentals layered in — not a pure commodity play.

modules.body: Buying mining or oil company stocks gives commodity-linked exposure with corporate fundamentals layered in — not a pure commodity play.

quiz.question: A futures-based commodity ETF may diverge from spot because:
options: It rolls contracts and pays fees

quiz.question: An ETN’s risk includes:
quiz.explanation: You are exposed to the note issuer.

quiz.question: Buying an oil major’s stock gives:
Expense ratios:
options: Drag long-run returns and vary by fund

quiz.question: Physical gold ETFs hold:
options: Bullion or claims per prospectus — verify

sections.title: OPEC and cartels
sections.content: OPEC+ coordinates production targets among major oil exporters. When members comply, prices can stabilize or rise; cheating or political fractures undermine agreements. Sanctions also reroute oil flows.

modules.body: OPEC+ coordinates production targets among major oil exporters. When members comply, prices can stabilize or rise; cheating or political fractures undermine agreements. Sanctions also reroute oil flows.

sections.title: Strategic reserves
sections.content: Countries maintain petroleum reserves to buffer emergencies. Releases can temporarily lower prices but do not fix structural shortages.

modules.body: Countries maintain petroleum reserves to buffer emergencies. Releases can temporarily lower prices but do not fix structural shortages.

sections.title: Non-OPEC supply
sections.content: U.S. shale, Norway, Brazil, and others add non-OPEC supply that responds to price with lags — technology and financing matter.

modules.body: U.S. shale, Norway, Brazil, and others add non-OPEC supply that responds to price with lags — technology and financing matter.

quiz.question: OPEC+ decisions matter because:
options: They influence collective oil supply targets

quiz.question: Cartel cohesion can weaken when:
options: Members cheat on quotas or face domestic budget stress

quiz.question: Sanctions can:
options: Shift oil flows between regions and affect prices
quiz.explanation: Barrels find routes — at a cost.

quiz.question: Shale supply often responds to:
options: Price signals with drilling and financing lags
quiz.explanation: High prices eventually attract capital — with delay.

sections.title: Correlation over time
sections.content: Commodities and equities are not perfectly correlated — correlation rises in some crises when everything sells off (liquidity crunch) and diverges during supply shocks that hurt growth but raise energy prices.

modules.body: Commodities and equities are not perfectly correlated — correlation rises in some crises when everything sells off (liquidity crunch) and diverges during supply shocks that hurt growth but raise energy prices.

sections.title: Inflation hedging
sections.content: Commodities can help when inflation surprises — but they are volatile and can lag policy responses. Size positions thoughtfully.

modules.body: Commodities can help when inflation surprises — but they are volatile and can lag policy responses. Size positions thoughtfully.

sections.title: Portfolio role
sections.content: Think of commodities as a satellite allocation: potential diversification and inflation sensitivity, not a core replacement for diversified equities and bonds for most investors.

modules.body: Think of commodities as a satellite allocation: potential diversification and inflation sensitivity, not a core replacement for diversified equities and bonds for most investors.

quiz.question: During a liquidity crisis, many assets may:
options: Correlate upward as investors raise cash
quiz.explanation: Correlation is not stable in stress.

quiz.question: Commodities as an inflation hedge are:
A satellite allocation means:
options: Smaller supportive slice around a core portfolio

sections.title: Why risk management matters
sections.content: Markets are uncertain; outcomes are path-dependent. Risk management exists so one bad streak does not end your ability to participate. Professionals obsess about survival first, returns second — because without capital, there is no compounding.

modules.body: Markets are uncertain; outcomes are path-dependent. Risk management exists so one bad streak does not end your ability to participate. Professionals obsess about survival first, returns second — because without capital, there is no compounding.

sections.title: Process over prediction
sections.content: You cannot control whether the next trade wins; you can control position sizing, entry rules, and how you respond to losses. A repeatable process is the only durable edge for most participants.

modules.body: You cannot control whether the next trade wins; you can control position sizing, entry rules, and how you respond to losses. A repeatable process is the only durable edge for most participants.

sections.title: Expectancy
sections.content: Long-run results approximate average win × win rate minus average loss × loss rate (simplified). Negative expectancy cannot be fixed by doubling down — only by improving the strategy or quitting.

modules.body: Long-run results approximate average win × win rate minus average loss × loss rate (simplified). Negative expectancy cannot be fixed by doubling down — only by improving the strategy or quitting.

quiz.question: The main goal of risk management is often:
options: Stay in the game and limit ruin risk

quiz.question: You control:
options: Position size and adherence to rules

quiz.question: Doubling down after losses without edge:
Expectancy depends on:
options: The full distribution of wins and losses
quiz.explanation: Size of wins/losses matters as much as frequency.

quiz.question: A process-based approach means:
quiz.explanation: Discipline scales better than hero trades.

sections.title: The 1–2% idea
sections.content: A common guideline is to risk only a small fraction of account equity on a single trade — often 1–2% — so a string of losses cannot wipe you out. The exact number should fit your strategy’s win rate and payoff ratio.

modules.body: A common guideline is to risk only a small fraction of account equity on a single trade — often 1–2% — so a string of losses cannot wipe you out. The exact number should fit your strategy’s win rate and payoff ratio.

sections.title: Risk per trade vs stop distance
sections.content: Position size links stop distance to dollar risk: tighter stops allow larger share counts for the same dollar risk — but tight stops get hunted in noise. Calibrate stops to volatility, not wishful thinking.
sections.shortTitle: Risk per trade vs stop distance

modules.body: Position size links stop distance to dollar risk: tighter stops allow larger share counts for the same dollar risk — but tight stops get hunted in noise. Calibrate stops to volatility, not wishful thinking.

sections.title: Leverage
sections.content: Leverage multiplies both gains and losses — and margin calls can force exits at the worst time. Treat leverage as a risk amplifier, not a free tool.

modules.body: Leverage multiplies both gains and losses — and margin calls can force exits at the worst time. Treat leverage as a risk amplifier, not a free tool.

quiz.question: Risking 1% per trade means:
options: Each trade risks ~1% of equity (by common definition)

quiz.question: Wider stops generally require:
options: Smaller position size for the same dollar risk
quiz.explanation: Risk = size × per-unit move to stop.

quiz.question: Position sizing should reflect:
quiz.explanation: Match size to strategy stats and market conditions.

quiz.question: A losing streak with proper sizing should:
options: Still leave capital to continue if rules were sound
Always mean the strategy is wrong immediately

sections.title: Stop losses
sections.content: A stop order exits a position at a market order once a price level trades — it caps loss but can be whipsawed in volatile names. Stops are insurance, not perfection.

modules.body: A stop order exits a position at a market order once a price level trades — it caps loss but can be whipsawed in volatile names. Stops are insurance, not perfection.

sections.title: Take profits
sections.content: Taking profits locks in gains — but cutting winners too early can forfeit trend upside. Some traders scale out: partial profits at targets, runner for trends.

modules.body: Taking profits locks in gains — but cutting winners too early can forfeit trend upside. Some traders scale out: partial profits at targets, runner for trends.

sections.title: Plan before entry
sections.content: Define stop and target logic before you enter — when you are calm. In the trade, emotions argue for moving stops; pre-commitment resists that.

modules.body: Define stop and target logic before you enter — when you are calm. In the trade, emotions argue for moving stops; pre-commitment resists that.

quiz.question: A stop-loss order primarily:
options: Guarantees fill at exactly the stop price in all conditions
Triggers a market order after the stop level — slippage possible

quiz.question: Take-profit rules help by:
options: Removing some emotional exit decisions in advance

quiz.question: Whipsaw risk means:
options: Stops can exit before a favorable move continues

quiz.question: Trailing stops:
options: Move with favorable price to protect gains
quiz.explanation: Lock in progress while allowing trend participation.

sections.title: Losses hurt more than gains help
sections.content: Loss aversion is well documented: a dollar lost feels worse than a dollar gained feels good. Revenge trading tries to “make it back” immediately — usually enlarging mistakes.
sections.shortTitle: Losses hurt more than gains help

modules.body: Loss aversion is well documented: a dollar lost feels worse than a dollar gained feels good. Revenge trading tries to “make it back” immediately — usually enlarging mistakes.

sections.title: Cooldown routines
sections.content: After a loss, step away, journal what happened, and return only when rules are clear. Reducing trade frequency after tilt preserves capital.

modules.body: After a loss, step away, journal what happened, and return only when rules are clear. Reducing trade frequency after tilt preserves capital.

sections.title: Identity
sections.content: If your self-worth is tied to each trade’s outcome, variance becomes unbearable. Treat trading as a probabilistic game with bounded risk — not a verdict on your value.

modules.body: If your self-worth is tied to each trade’s outcome, variance becomes unbearable. Treat trading as a probabilistic game with bounded risk — not a verdict on your value.

quiz.question: Revenge trading usually:
options: Increases size and risk after losses

quiz.question: A trading journal helps because:
options: It forces review of decisions independent of P&L luck

quiz.question: Loss aversion implies:
options: People feel losses more strongly than equivalent gains

quiz.question: After a large loss, a healthy step is:
options: Cooldown and review rules before next trade
